speaker: Sam Schrager
words: John B. Miller has not lived in Bovill since he was a young man. Most of his working career has been with Standard Oil of California as a field specialist in exploration geology. Yet his interest in the early life of Bovill has endured, and after a great deal of research in his spare time, he published The Trees Grew Tall in December of 1972. The book is beautifully written and photographed, and is highly regarded by old timers who remember those days well. In this conversation, John Miller touches on the writing of The Trees Grew Tall, early life in Bovill, and problems that society has today.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: John Miller was very aware of it. Back in 1928 we were back in Wisconsin. In 1928 and 1929 we spent a winter back there. We used to get this Bovill high school paper here, and they published a two -page resume of the history of Bovill that they'd gotten from T .P. Jones. My dad was going through that and picking out things that he thought were wrong in it. As all old timers do, every time anybody mentions a history, or published a history, the old timers always start to pick it to pieces.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: He started to do that, plus embellish it with a lot of other things that he said ought to be mentioned. I'd always heard him talking about the pioneer days and the early things and so forth. I had quite a background in it. I think at that time I began to get aware of it, plus the fact that I was always very much interested in old photographs. They always intrigued me.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I guess it grew out of that.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: When were you born and how old were you then?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I was born in 1912. I was about 16 at that time.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Any folks that moved from here?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: They'd moved from Bovill. We'd just gone east to visit some of his folks. When we got back there, his brothers talked us into staying over winter. He wanted to spend the winter back there. My folks decided to stay. We rented a house. Among us, we picked up work to support ourselves over winter. We spent the winter there. We moved back the next year. That's when we moved from the place just south of town here. We already owned the little meadow out here southeast of town. We also owned this 47 acres south of the treating plant. We had a house on that, but we rented that out. When we came back, we moved on to little meadow out southeast of town here and built a house there.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that it just continued to grow? Your interest in history as you grew up?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Oh yes, I think it did. I suppose I was always interested in history. History was interesting to me as a small child. But a small child doesn't quite realize he's living among history and that his own small community has a history too. This doesn't quite come to you until you suddenly discover that things are getting a long ways behind. You know things or heard about things that other people or younger people haven't experienced and known. It suddenly comes through.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I don't really believe that pioneers in general are very much aware of history. They're so tied up in the present.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: When a community begins to develop a little past, people are going to start to get fascinated with it. That's the trend I see in the present day people. That's why I think people are so interested when somebody does sit down and write up something that is readable.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Could you tell how you went about collecting the material that made up The Trees Grew Tall?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Just briefly.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Haphazardly. I had no experience in it at all. I suppose if I didn't have a framework, a thread of a story, of the sequence of things in mind, it would have been a terrific task.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Would you like a cup of coffee?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I did have a framework and that gives you something to hang something on. It also serves as a filter for when you hear something. If it fits in, well of course if it's true it always fits in. But when you start talking to people you hear of so many things that you are just unaware of completely. It begins to fill that framework in pretty fast.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Do you use sugar cream?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Not me.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: You think I warm up this doughnut? He doesn't drink coffee do you?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Not very much, I just got through it. And then he drank half of it.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Did you decide to write a book and then start gathering material or did you gather material first?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I would have to say a little about that in the introduction of the book here. I had started gathering material but perhaps not actually noting things down in note form. I had put several news items aside that had covered history and I had been already grabbing pictures whenever I had a chance. Whenever there was a chance for me to get a hold of a picture that was of historical interest I had put away. Either clipping out of a newspaper or in some instance an actual photograph. To this extent I had gathered some material. When I started working on the book I had no intention really, my original plan had never really been to write a book but to do something in a much smaller scale of the type of thing that you people are interested in. Just getting the information and recording it and putting it away someplace where somebody else could get a hold of it before it was lost. It just seemed to me such a shame that all this information was being lost because it was in people's memory and nobody was getting it down.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Are there certain people that you depended on in particular for information? If there were, why were they such good informants for you?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Only to the extent that I knew who the old people were around town that were associated with the town in the early days. I knew them all and they were the natural people to go to.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: My facilities to reach people out of Bovill were rather limited because it takes time. You have to go a hundred miles to talk to somebody and you can probably talk to three or four people locally while you're doing that. Since I had a pretty limited time budget I had to think of that.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I did go to a few of those people and I wrote letters to others and I sent inquiries to a few through friends to settle certain issues. It was pretty apparent after I had done a couple of stints up in the Bovill area, interviewing people, that I pretty much had enough material so far as volume was concerned to do me. The main thing was to verify as much as I could and settle on dates.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There were so many discrepancies.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: How did you go about resolving conflicts?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: As much as I could. I finally had to go into the university library and dig out old newspapers and things like that.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Gwen Lawrence's diary was her mother's diary that she had used and she had compiled this into a manuscript for a book. I had access to that settlement. There were a great many things. I went into the library stacks down at the university to scrounge out more data.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: In odds and ends there were a lot of things that there wasn't a way to really resolve. Very often on those things I reported in such a way that my wording is not that specific about small details. I didn't give dates sometimes or when I told something as a story I told it as a story rather than necessarily an absolute fact. Or I mention a question that someday is questionable at times. Nevertheless I run into challenges all the time on the veracity of what I've said. I feel in many instances it comes to this nitpicking type of criticism that I've mentioned.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: It often hinges on the fact that one person remembers things differently than another. I'm very well aware that the day after something happens you can ask two people that listen to what happened and you don't get the same story. After fifty or sixty years it's kind of easy to understand why it might happen.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you think this is the most difficult thing about trying to capture the past of a small place?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: It's very difficult to interview people and actually come up with precisely what happened at any time. This is a precise thing about the order of events. This is what they are very often confused about. This is the order of events. While I was interviewing on these railroads for example, I had a spread of about three years that nobody seemed to be sure what happened. They couldn't agree on the year that the railroads were completed for example. I knew pretty well, but still you begin to question your own information when you hear old timers say, Well, no, that wasn't so. Something else was so. Until I could get papers that actually showed to prove the dates of completion. You have to wonder if you might not be wrong about something.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: For example, the first meeting we held over here at the church, they argued around about this and so on and so forth. They said, Well, the excursion train came in here in 1910. Well, no it didn't. I rode it in in 1907. When you start going back into history and inquiring what happened, there were two excursion trains, one in each year. One of them commemorating the completion of the W, I, and M railroad through and the second one commemorating the junction of the two railroads when Will Waffie completed their line. Both stories are true.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: You said you already had established, like talking about the filter, you already had established some idea yourself of what had happened.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: How much did this idea have to change through that sort of interaction?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There had to be changes. There were things that I had in my mind that were probably basically correct in context but were shifted off in time or were a little wrong in some part of the details. As I would have told the details, they wouldn't have been wrong. I mean, from my own recollection, they wouldn't have been out of contact with what happened, but they would not have been entirely factual. These are the things you have to check on. It's nearly factual as you can. But as I say, it does form a framework to fill in. You're surprised at how fast it does fill in when you start interviews. But the time is taken then when you run into discrepancies. It's also filling in. I found that I was quite unskilled at interviewing people and getting the first interview, getting all the detail I wanted. They'd go over the information. For example, I didn't have a tape recorder. I'd try to sit there and take notes and I'd end up with the notes. I'd go back and start to write something about it and I'd find I missed two or three names. Well, they mentioned names there. I don't have those names in my notes. It would be much better if I had the names. So I'd have to backtrack to people and ask them again. I had that sort of thing happening all the time.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you have thoughts from your study and your own experience here about the character of the Bovill area, about what's unique to it?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Well, I don't really know that there's anything so unique about it. Among these communities back in this part of Latah County, of course, it tucked back in the woods farther. It depended almost entirely on timber rather than on farming. As you go westward, the country becomes more and more dependent on farming and less and less on its trees. The Bovill community, of course, depended almost totally on the forest as a source of occupation. Another thing is compared with towns such as St. Mary's and other towns of the North that are fundamentally logging communities. Of course, Bovill was a Potlatch town. It was established because of the force of the Potlatch company rather than other logging interests.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: It was a juncture point for the railroads, and that was very important to the town. Of course, Elk River was a mill town, Potlatch was a mill town. It was a town that was primarily set up to move logs. It was really in the prime part of the White Pine, I think the best White Pine in almost all North Idaho. There were a lot of White Pines in North Idaho, but there was none of it that equaled the sands that were around Bovill.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: So those are certain things that characterized this town. The fact that more logging crews moved out of Bovill than any other town. There were not even much operations around Elk River and in Deary that were Bovill men involved, much more than there were Elk River and Deary people involved in Bovill. This was just a kind of a natural center for all the area.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that, how would you draw the, could you draw differences between the way people were in Bovill as compared to a farming town like Hendrick or Genesee, just besides the fact that here they log and there they farm? Would you make for a different kind of a society?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I'm sure that the logger fundamentally has a different attitude than the farmer.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Any time that you go into the family people, the old time family people were conservative people in general. But the farmer by nature is much more conservative and careful. He has to become much more business -like in his attitude or he'll go broke. He has to run a business. A logger of course is working for a monthly wage and he's got a steady income and he knows just about what it will do. He lives in this way. He is not forced as a farmer as to maybe work 14 -15 hours. He's on a regular work schedule and he has more free time at night. The logger very often, maybe he was engaged in a town and worked right here in a community and lived at home but many of these people of course spend their time, their nights in a lumber camp, excepting perhaps weekends with the family, those who lived worked out in the camps. This is quite a different life than the farmer has.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Loggers, especially the old time lumberjacks, have a really strong reputation for their tall tales and their pictures being real hell raisers. That sort of image surrounds them.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that was a fairly accurate representation?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I think it probably overstressed. Mrs. Wonky down here was showing me the book that came out. It's one of the North Idaho towns that deals with something about cock boots. I got the impression from it that it tended to be a sensational side. They tend to over -characterize the lumberjack as a rough man. The lumberjack was a hard drinking man, there's no doubt of it. They used lots of liquor. They lived back in the lumber camps until they were really bent to get out and let off steam and they'd head for Spokane or something and do it. Still, they weren't really that rough. Storybooks are inclined to picture them. Many times you can engage people in a hard and dangerous physical work. I'm sure you have people who have a rough point of view about things. As I say, I think that part has been overstressed in books.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: They were tranches to a large extent. They were largely bachelors, excepting for these key men who lived in areas like Bow. They were family members. The typical lumberjack in the lumber camp was a transient bachelor. I would say probably two quarters of the crew.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much contact in this thing with what lumberjacks thought of themselves?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I know the whole thing about the outside looking lumberjack has this image of a rough and ready sort of...

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: What did the lumberjack think of his own job, his own self, his own relationship to the company?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Probably the man that ought to answer a question like that would be somebody like Homer Pelton, who was one and who worked closer to them, or a man like Jerry Carlin who lived down the street because they knew these people a lot better than I do.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I have the impression that the lumberjack, like almost every class of people, have a pretty good opinion of their own image. If they are rough and ready people, it's because they admire people who are rough and ready and like to fit into the image.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I didn't know of very many fights among the lumberjacks, for example. They're pretty amiable people. They get along well together. They like each other.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: They appreciate that each man appreciates that the other man is a hard worker. They like that he's tough and can get out and take it and do a man's share and he can swear as good as anything and he can drink as hard as any and so forth. These are the images that they aspire to.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: They try to fit the pattern as well as they can, but I don't really think they enjoy brawling and that sort of thing any more than anybody else does. Occasionally, somebody gets drunk and probably gets in a fight.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: But no man is going to go around with a chip in his shoulder and be spoiling for a fight all the time and be very popular. When you talk about somebody rough and ready, as much as anything, I think you're probably talking about his ability to get out and do a hard day's work and not complain and take all kinds of rough knocks on the job and get along well with the people around him and behave pretty much like they do. I think this is really what your... The life is a rough and ready life. That's kind of what it boils down to.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I have this picture of this man they call the lumberjack preacher, Dick Farrell. I suspect that one of the reasons he was admired so much is that he had been a pugilist. The lumberjacks followed an awful lot of them. They followed the professional prizefighters and admired them. They were physical men and the lumberjacks appreciate physical men. Here was a guy that had been a prizefighter and he turned preacher. It was probably much more interesting to him because he was a prizefighter. Not only that, he was a good rough and tumble fighter. They knew that here was a preacher that would come along and look any man in camp. My brother -in -law was kind of the same way, Billy Mush. He had been a prizefighter in his younger days. In the average lumber camp that he worked, I don't think there would be a man in the crew that could lick him. I never knew him to fight anybody. When he went to work for the CCC camps out here, he gave me instructions to the young kids. He was a man then close to 50 years old. He had the fastest hands ever saw. Gosh, he could leave those hands. He would pour the hotcakes out on the grid like that and go along with the flipper. Just a series of movements up and down with his hands. As fast as that hand would move up and down with the hotcake turner, he would get under the edge of the hotcake and cut it over. He would flip over 60 hotcakes in about 60 seconds. He would have precision hands. When he worked for the CCC out here, he gave these boxing instructions. We used to pick up these fellows and haul them out to camp once in a while when we would be driving out that way. I remember my dad talking to one of the boys. He was telling him how they had boxing teams on the CCC camp. He said, Billy Musch is our boxing coach. Dad said, yes, he used to be a pretty good boxer when he was young. If he said, yes, there isn't anybody in camp he can't slap now if he wants to. But of course, he had been a pro boxer and he would get those hands through there and you just couldn't stop him.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: A person who wasn't of that category, he didn't have the stamina to fight anymore or anything like that. Those hands were just as vicious as they ever were.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that pretty definite lines were drawn between the guys who lived in camps and were transient loggers and the people who were loggers and with families here? Was there much of a distinction between the both of them?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Well, of course, the loggers were just back and forth through here. The tranches were back and forth through. They often had good friends in town. Usually did. An awful lot of them, at least, did have good friends in town. They knew the local people and the local people knew them by and large. There was a tendency for the same crews to come back to the same area. There would be some filtering back and shifting forth between lumber camps. An awful lot of the loggers get to liking an area and they like a certain boss and they go back there. So, even though they are in and out, they'll quit their job and go up to Spokane and blow off their steam and spend their money, but very often they came back to the same area to work.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Would a lot of the transient men stick around when the camps were closed during the mud season or during the bad season? Would they stick around?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Some of them did. There were places.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: For example, the boarding houses and hotels here. The big ones, of course, were the Bovill Hotel and the Spokane Hotel and the David Hotel.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I have the impression, I don't know whether this is quite true all the way along the lines, but I think that by and large the David Hotel and the Bovill Hotel catered more to rather long -term borders. People who would maybe stay over winter or working out of Bovill might go out on jobs out of town, but they poured in town. Whereas the Spokane Hotel, get rid of Kevin's noise here, I guess.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Kevin, can you play inside and watch your TV or something?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There were an awful lot more overnighters.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: They were just in town for a night or two and headed out. But then there were old people like Pat Malone, for example, who boarded all full -time at the Spokane Hotel.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: But, yes, there were places here where Lumberjack stayed for fairly long -terms, but many of them did go out over winter. But the camps ran during the winter a lot. Perhaps more than now, because you have to consider that these heavy machines that they're using today, rubber -tired log haulers or even a tractor, you get it out and work in deep snow and in mud and so forth, in trucks particularly, and then will mire down. Well, in the old days, everything was on rails. And the mud didn't bother the teams so much. As a matter of fact, as far as the mud teams are concerned, the mud up to a skidding log is a good lubricant. And snow is also a good lubricant. In heavy snow, they could use sleighs and haul logs on sleighs, so they can skid through the snow much better than they can get out in deep snow at this equipment.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: So they ran in the winter a great deal.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There's one guy that I've talked to who said that he quit logging when they went to the more seasonal logging when they started getting the heavier equipment because he couldn't work as long as he used to be able to before.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: What about the difference in morals between the lumberjack and the family man? Was that a problem in the community or was it dealt with?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I can't remember anything like that was a problem, excepting, of course, in those days drinking was something of a problem. In most of that period, drinking was, in the years of prohibition, was against the law and possession of liquor was against the law. So there was always a conflict between the drinking lumberjack and the law from that standpoint.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: What about the difference in morals between the two?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I was probably too young to appreciate altogether the aspects of the moral life of the lumberjack otherwise, but I suspect for his women, he generally went to Spokane. There were plenty of them available up there and in the small town they just aren't very available. You do talk about houses, at least for a period of time. That was in the saloon days and of course it was legal at that time. So far as I know,

speaker: John B. Miller
words: the open prostitution disappeared when the saloons went out of business. It's a little strange because it ties together serving liquor and prostitution. I don't suppose there is any town hardly, but you will find a small percentage of women.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I don't actually know.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: The picture that you paint of Pat Malone has really been borne out by people that I've talked to who agree. In fact, Pat's ideas about having a good time and drinking himself, which makes it seem that the prohibition was sort of a farce out here.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Well, it certainly was. Pat Malone was probably the most colorful guy that ever hit the town. He was a character and there would never be another like him. Probably. Just that sort of a situation.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I've been trying to figure out who he ever did arrest. Because everybody I've talked to seems to have been a good friend of his and went out drinking with him. I think he preferred it that way.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: When he made an arrest, I think somebody was pushing him. He better get busy.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Yes, you'll find the town is full of stories about Pat Malone and the problems could never come to the end of them. If you had gotten into town twenty years ago, you'd heard twenty times as many because there were twenty -nine times as many people that knew old Pat as there are now exist. He was just the character that people most liked to laugh about. But it didn't mean at all that they didn't like him. I think the actual value of the community was largely ignored because it didn't fit the image of people like Pat. Since he was a kind of a laughable character, he was going to have that image. I don't imagine that people would have had nearly so much to laugh about old Pat if they weren't on it. He hadn't been in the law enforcement side of things and obliged to at least make token manifestations of doing something.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: With regard to liquor, since people were going to drink anyway, they just made as much fun out of it as they could. He was part of the fun.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: What do you think his value was?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: The presence of such a man does stabilize a community. If anything ever does happen that the law needs teeth, people will back up the man who has the authority. Knowing that this is so, it keeps things in hand.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you recall any situations like that?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I recall times when Pat made arrests. I do know also that Pat, to a certain extent, helped the town keep control of such laws as the herd law. They would pick up cows if they were wandering in the streets, which was illegal, and put them in the pond and there would be a fine. He enforced that sort of thing.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I told in my book about one arrest he made out here at Camp 7. There were other arrests that he made. I know where the offenses were less severe. I know minor theft and things like that. I don't recollect names at the time, but I do recall. I'm aware that things like that happen from time to time.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There was the instance out here where they had the fight in the cedar yard. The two men fought and one of them was killed. The rest is needed. The presence of the deputy sheriff makes it possible. More than anything, in the very early days when the saloons were here, there was more rough and tough fighting. Drunkenness is common in the streets. There are many drunks in the streets. After prohibition, the drunks generally try to become inconspicuous. They try to stay off the street. When there's no prohibition, they're out in the street. When you get a bunch of drunks together, you're going to have trouble. Pat would haul them away to jail if it got too bad. He wasn't a firm man. He wasn't a man who would dominate the people. But still, by and large, the people are law -abiding people. If he needed help, these men were his friends. If one man got out of hand and Pat needed to haul them away, it was somebody to help.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Basically, what you're saying is that he interpreted the law himself. He interpreted the things that were necessary to have a legal...

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I suspect that if he had been a club -swinging cop, or a gun -toting cop, that tried to handle the law in a hard -fisted way, he might have run into more trouble than he did just persuading people.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I've heard young people mention that they were afraid of Pat. It never occurred to me to be afraid of Pat. But young people... just the fact that he was a policeman, he had authority, and that was enough. There's very little doubt that fear of the law is probably part of respect for the law, at least for some people. He would probably go to hand -in -hand to some extent. But he was such a mild -mannered fellow that it never occurred to me to be afraid of him. It didn't occur to me to be doing the type of thing that I had to fear him.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I did hear one instance... I don't recall the individuals. I know Davy Ellison was one of them that was mentioned that was involved in this. There was one time when some of the kids got fairly noisy, and somebody tried to quiet them down with very little effect.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Pat generally didn't take a hand in such things, but in this case he did step up among the boys and said, quiet down. They just shut up right there. The law had spoken, so that was it.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There's a psychology there, I think, from that standpoint. I think he served the town's needs to a large degree. It sounds like he really understood the town's needs and responded to them. I doubt if he did. I just think he was a kind of an easy -going, affable man. I don't think he understood the hardness of authority. I don't think he was a tough man at all. But he was a man whose habits, his way of dress, his way of talk, his way of living was very much like the lumberjack's way.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I suspect that the fact that he was affable made him much better liked among the lumberjacks than if he had tried to be hard -handed.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: They didn't pay much attention to him most of the time. They knew they didn't have to. They could do what they liked to do.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: If they got too far out of line and he spoke up, if somebody was too drunk to listen to him, why not?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: He was an authority and kind of a natural leader at that point. The friends would come down the row. I think it probably worked in that type of people. It probably worked better than if he had actually tried to handle a guy.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: You seem to have a special feeling for Pete Olsen in the book.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Was that a feeling a lot of people in the community had about Pete?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Yes, I think Pete was another one of the people that you could call a real character. People looked at him as a character. He was kind of off by himself in many ways. He was comical in many ways.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: He was certainly a good citizen. He certainly had a wonderful mind, no doubt about it. People realized that he could remember things and be more accurate in many ways in his memory. He was better informed by far than a lot of people with possibly much more formal education than Pete had. I don't really know what Pete's formal education was, but he was not short -suited. He certainly must have been up in some place in the high school grade. He had a mind that acquired information. He had a curious mind and a very retentive memory. He was an ardent reader of anything and everything. He admired knowledge, but he had very quaint ways.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: His place is right up there. That's his farm, the old slaughterhouse set up in the middle of it. That's where they butchered the meat in the older days. Pete acquired it somewhat later than that. In that period, he was just a logger. He acquired that place and it became his farm. You'd see old Pete up there at 12 o 'clock at night making hay by moonlight. Certainly, that's just not good. That goes counter to the old saying, make hay while the sun shines. He made hay all night long. If the dew didn't get too heavy,

speaker: John B. Miller
words: he never fit the conventional pattern hardly in anything. I don't know how straight he came here from Sweden. He did come into the community from Sweden, I think probably around 1910. I never knew the exact date.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Incidentally, this is one of the things I found out from the Moore family. I had thought the Hayes family came in here about 1909. I found out from Mrs. Moore's record down in Potlatch yesterday. They were here at least in 1970. At least two years earlier than I thought.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Pete evidently liked the United States from the start and became a very good citizen. He was a volunteer in the U .S. Army. I doubt if there was a more patriotic man in the whole town than Pete Olson. He was a man who regarded himself more as an American.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was a general attitude towards Potlatch forests in the community?

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Was it very individual?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I think the people here are tough on the workmen.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There's going to be some antagonism.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: People continue to work for them.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: A great many of them admired the bosses and whatnot that they worked under.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Still there's awareness that the company has got lots of money and they're making good money. They're carrying on with policies that the workmen feel are not to his best interest. The fact that it's been worse in the past leaves the employee with a feeling that he only makes progress when he demands better things. The company isn't ready to give him anything.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There has to be some truth to this.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I doubt in the struggle of the working man against big business that the working man would have ever made and gotten very far if he hadn't struggled.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I'm not very much a friend of the unions. They probably do us more harm than good in the present world. In the past they have done us a lot of good.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: The IWWs, for example, were the beginning union among the loggers here. They were hated by many of the people who regarded themselves to be the more law and order type.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: My dad,

speaker: John B. Miller
words: the worst thing he could call a man would be an IWW. He thought that was the bottom of the well. I guess my family in Dearie, my uncle and aunt, must have had about the same opinion. I was talking to my cousin in Dearie last year when I had this manuscript ready. She was reading about the IWWs and her father, Emory Olsen, had been a clerk out in Camp Sex during World War I when the wobbly trouble was bad. He'd been deputized by the Sheriff's office over in Moscow to make arrests and to conduct the suspects to Moscow. He was held by the Sheriff and she was remembering that her father used to come through every once in a while. Somebody he'd picked up had been an accused IWW member. He'd pick him up in camp and bring him through and perhaps stop at home for a few minutes and maybe give him a meal or something. The fellow would sit down in one of the family chairs someplace. Afterwards, the girls were almost afraid to sit in the chair because they'd been contaminated by an IWW.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: This is one place where you'll find an awful lot of discrepant opinions. First, about the worth of IWWs. Second, about what acts they did. For example, I tell about this dropping a boom across a pole pile in the cedar yard here by a man who'd been newly hired. It was his first day on the job. They put him on the loader and the first thing he did was drop the boom across the pole pile. There are two interpretations. One is that he was trying to sabotage the company. The second is that he was just an inept operator.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Which story is right?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I don't know, really. My family always believed he was an IWW because this was a typical IWW type of thing. They were out to do this when they could. They were out to hurt the companies. The companies were all bad and you hurt them whenever you can. Not all IWW members were out doing damage by any means. This is surely true. A lot of them were in the union because they felt it was beneficial to them and they would not do anything unethical. But there was some sabotage and in the Bovill area, so far as I know, it was not very bad. There were fires that sprang up in the woods that were late IWWs. I'm sure some of them had to be deliberately set just to cause trouble. There were many more grain field fires in that place. People would go through and set up grain fields on fire. Farmers needed us. They were afraid of what they were up to. But it only takes one man in a hundred to sabotage. You have a lot of trouble.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that these sympathies one way or the other were like your family's or another family that might like the IWW? Were these things, did they stay private or did they sort of determine your friends or have to do with who your associates were?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: They always have something to do with who you like better than others.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Your good or bad opinion of people is always influenced a little bit by their sympathies with things like that. It's a little bit like this late pro -war and anti -war groups. This isn't the right wording because I don't think anybody was really pro -war as such.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: But let's say between the group that says stop the war and the other group that says let's get on with it and finish it. You have the two schools of sympathy and if somebody doesn't agree with you, they're not quite as nice as if they do.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: This kind of disagreement could be a pretty public thing. People would talk about it. It wasn't something like religion that you'd keep more to yourself.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: A little bit, but then doggone it, it only goes to a certain point. So and so down the street, the Culbertsons, I use that name because it isn't a bogle name, but they're IWWs. But you knew them long before they were IWWs. Maybe they're not IWWs because you very seldom know whether they actually carry a card. These cards were carried secretly. But you know that they are sympathizers. But you knew these people a long time and you know that they're honest people and hardworking people.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: They're good friends, they're good neighbors. You're not going to think very bad of them in the long run. You wish they weren't sympathetic to IWWs. You can't mouse to that. But on the other hand, a tough lumberjack who's a stranger to you and you hear him expounding IWW talk, and if you're against it, why then you right away classify him. He's one of these so and so's. He can't be trusted.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: So it's a matter of degree and it's a matter of who he is and how well you know him to a certain extent.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Why would they want to antagonize the IWWs?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Again, I don't think that every IWW sympathizer, particularly certainly not in an area like Bogle where you're dealing with labor matters, labor disputes, they don't look deeply into it to know whether it's communist or not communist or care very much if it serves their purpose.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: But the communist doctrine actually is aimed at undermining the government.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: So that anything they could do, if you got among the real hardcore communists, anything that they could do to create disorder, to hurt the government in any kind of a backhanded way, they were ready to do. Now the average lumberjack who took part in this movement, he was more concerned with something else. He was living out in a lumber camp that was very inadequate for his needs. He was infested with bud bugs and lice and perhaps other things, rare men that he didn't much like. He didn't think he ought to put up with them. He didn't like the fact that there were no clean beds in the camps and no laundry facilities. If he wanted to wash his clothes, he had to go down, get an iron bucket of some kind and go down and bust a hole in the ice at the creek and dip out water and get firewood, build a fire and heat the water over the fire and laundry his clothes. And there was no place to dry them after he got them laundered and he tried to dry clothes in the middle of the winter without a facilities. He came in dirty and wet maybe after a stormy rainy day and his clothes were wet with rain and sweatful and so forth.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Wool smells bad when it's wet and it's clean and when it's dirty it smells ten times as bad. And here he is in a little narrow bunk shack with tiers of bunks on both sides of him and up the center of the aisle. They stretch a line and all the men hang their clothes on the line and they're about that far from your face and you're trying to sleep. Those things are stinking to high heaven. And there was nothing being done at all to clean up the beds or provide clean bedding or laundry facilities in camp or anything else. The company wasn't concerned with it. And he's in a conflict with the company, although the IWW is a means of getting back at the head of things and getting this thing put to rights.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: It went a long ways in accomplishing that.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I understand that there was at least one strike. I've been told there was a strike.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you know about that?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: And I've also been told the strike resulted in the change of conditions.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I heard about strikes in the logging woods and about the mills being stopped and things when I was young. And I'm not aware of where these things happened. And I questioned some of the locals here and they tell me that work was never stopped in the Bovill area due to strikes. And I'm not in a position to dispute it. I know in some parts of the Northwest there were some fairly serious toppings. There was also sabotage in the mills and things like that. The equipment was broken. I do know that the potluck company was very worried that they were going to suffer some severe losses due to, not only the strikes, but to... They were very much afraid of fires and damage of equipment and that sort of thing. But I don't know of instances where there was very much done, nothing serious in the woods around Bovill. I don't know whether the potluck mill ever might have been shut down, but if it was it was temporary. And it apparently didn't stop any of the logging operations.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I don't think the El Carrerean mill was ever shut down during the strike. But I have not investigated that point thoroughly. I have run into people who swear that it didn't happen. I think you might have to investigate it thoroughly. You might have to pick newspapers and other sources.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I've been told that Bovill is strong, democratic politically.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you know if that's true and if it is how that has come about?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: It was about election returns that someone said to me that it goes strong democratically.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I wouldn't be surprised if it's true today. Most so -called blue shirt communities, or communities where you have wage oriented people, do tend to go in the present day democratic. In the days of my boyhood, I think that Bovill went mostly republican. There was a strong swing toward them, democratic as there was nationwide in Roosevelt's time. I think there are relatively few communities where the livelihood is largely based on physical work or blue collar type work. That's a poor term for lumberjack because of you, plaid shirt.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I think when you have that kind of a wage base, ever since the depression years, I think there has been a tendency for a democratic vote. Today, the democrats nationwide outnumber republicans almost two to one. The only salvaging thing is there is an intervening group. The non -affiliated voters help to balance things out a little bit more. But I would guess they probably do, and I would guess it dates back to the depression years. But I haven't been in the community at voting time to really know the sentiments very well.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: What do you think has happened to Bovill in recent years, since you left? Do you come back and see what it's like now? Does that give you any ideas about where it might be headed?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: The area in general, so far as natural beauty is concerned, has been coming up in the last forty years. I think about the low point might have been about 1930 when the very hard logging of the prime timber in the near vicinity of Bovill was finished. Of course, the logging operations leave just an unbelievable litter and mess behind. Particularly that type of logging did leave the woods badly littered with broken trees and unburned litter of all types. Of course, that has been obliterated by time to a large extent, and you've got the new growth.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: It's getting large enough now to restore an awful lot of natural beauty. In the meantime, though, the town has run down just unbelievably. Bovill, I think, has the prettiest natural setting of any town, and it has a nice layout to the town. You come across the hill, and look at the town and the way it lays down here against the mountains and the background and so forth, and among the trees and so forth, it has a nice look. But nowadays, when you get down in the middle of the town and see how shoddy everything is, and so many of the houses have been let run down, and worse than all, you go down and see what happened to Main Street. The lower part of Main Street is just an absolute wreck. This is very depressing. Now, as to the future, I would suppose that the future may not look at times the beauty may be large by logging of the young timber, because the young timber is getting large enough so they're beginning to cut it. I feel they're cutting it prematurely. Evidently, the timber is needed that badly, and it is cheap enough to get the nearby small stuff out so that it can be done, and unfortunately, in our type of political situations set up. You can say this against capitalism, it's all profit -oriented. That means getting goods from the producer to the consumer as cheaply as possible. So even though it may not be good for the long -term benefit of the public to do something now, it might be much better to wait another twenty years to cut this stuff. They're cutting stuff now that's perhaps twelve to sixteen inches, and if they waited another twenty years, you'd be cutting stuff that might be fifteen to twenty inches in diameter.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: This would make a much better timber. In the meantime, there is still mature timber that is just over -mature and the bugs get in it and are ruining it. That stuff ought to be taken out. It ought to be taken out now before it goes bad. This stuff allowed to grow.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there's a general feeling in the old -timers about how the forests have been managed?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: It seems to me that everybody must be aware that there's some mismanagement.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I don't actually know what is the best way to handle forests. The present trend, of course, has been that, at least in some areas, they've gone on this strip -logging type thing. They just knocked down everything and leave nothing standing.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I'm not an expert enough in this kind of field to weigh one thing against another.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: In the old days, T .P. Jones' policy, for example, was to just set the brush and slash on fire and let it burn. If it burned some small stuff, quite fine, but usually some small stuff survived. If you go back over those areas today, most of those areas have grown up to a pretty nice stand of timber. Maybe there's a lot to be said for doing it that way. At the time, it was done for expediency. This was the cheapest way to get the timber out and to leave the area and get rid of its mess.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: These areas have grown up to a nice stand of fine timber. Just leveling everything to the ground may not be the best thing to do.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I think foresters should take a long, hard look at what they're doing. More than anything, they shouldn't be letting forest go to waste in one area just because it's cheaper to cut logs than another. This is the type of thing that's done too much.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: You get the same thing in mining. People go underground after some ore.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: You've got some low grade areas and high grade areas that really pays to mine the high grade. So you bypass the low grade and then you perhaps never can afford to go back after it.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: You're going to leave metal in the ground that is going to be badly needed in the future. Certain parts of the body run to zinc and others to copper. There's good sale for copper so you leave the zinc behind. This is not economical in the long term because all of these metals are going to be scarce.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: We're on this now so much interested in the environment.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: But our economics has been set up to buy the product from the man who can sell it the cheapest. He goes out and ruins the land to get the product to market. Two dollars a ton cheaper while his product is bought. This is the way our system works and it's not right. It's a short term thing.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: How would that change?

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Is it an ethic that people have to have or is it government that would have to change the situation?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: The public viewpoint on this is changing but I'm not sure it's changing in an educated way. I wonder if people understand. They want to do things better. But I do feel that there's probably in the background of the average individual there's not enough knowledge about the economics of things, about resources and their nature. There are too many people with the idea that the earth's resources, oil and metals and things like that, are limitless. As you use up what you've got now you'll find new ways to get more. This only has a degree of truth in it. But it's certainly not entirely true. Too many of these things once they're gone, they're gone forever. Unlike the forests which are going to be grown again.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There is a break over point where it is no longer feasible to go in and mine low grade ore for example. For one thing, the lower grade the oil the more energy it's going to take to process it in the metal. The lower grade the ore the bigger machine it is to get it out to move it economically. You get to a break over point where you can't afford to operate it anymore for the metal you extract, it's not going to work. You're using up energy that you can't afford in a situation in which the energy supply is diminishing.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Can you replace the energy source?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Nobody knows yet.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Can you replace it with something new?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I don't think anybody knows for sure.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: With regard to oil, even if you could replace the energy, what do you do for lubricants?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: You run into that kind of a problem.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: You can't help but use your raw materials but you ought to use them with an eye to the future. You shouldn't be using them just with a degree of how do I do something the cheapest and use yesterday and to heck with the future. It shouldn't be done in that way. That's the way we operate.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: It seems to be sort of a frontier mentality that people are realizing it's no longer viable.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: They're realizing it's no longer viable but I don't think many people realize the degree or in some ways even the proximity of possible disaster in this area. You stop to think that in metals for example, you know how much was used up in the late war alone. It was tremendous. And yet since the war, we have used more metals than the world has used more metals than it has in all the previous world's history, including the war. And you are going up on that exponential curve all the time.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: How long can this happen? How long can we exist on an expansion economy?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: We think we have to expand the economy by something like 5 % per year in order to have a viable economy. This is disaster. You can't do this. There isn't a supply of material. You're in the wrong material of anything that can take care of the type of demand you'd have ten years from now on this exponential curve. It's mathematically an impossibility. And if you can proceed in this way, the end is the disaster just as inevitable as death. It's the only thing that can happen.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: This makes me think of the past again because it reminds me of what you say at the end of the book.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: At the very end you say were it possible to go back?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There is little doubt. It would be a rush. It does seem like a lot of old timers do remember the past as being better.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: It makes me wonder, do you think that we idealize the past as compared to the present? Is it because the people that are old now are young men and vigorous? Or was life really fuller then and not faced with these kinds of problems?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I think that in the older type of life there probably was more satisfaction in a way. Part of what I talked about I guess, part of what I had in mind is the sentiments.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I think it's very natural for people to be sentimental about the past.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: In terms of the American people I think there is a tendency to be sentimental about pioneer things. Their attachment for, for example, antiques reflects this mentality. There is a desire also to get back to the old conditions of availability of fish and game, for example. That appeals to a lot of people. There are just all kinds of motives that are behind that.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: The old life appeals to a lot of people even though they know that it was a rough life. If they got back and had to live that kind of life I'm not sure they would want to stay there very long, but still the idea appeals to them.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There was a contentment about it. There's no doubt about it. The life was tranquil,

speaker: John B. Miller
words: fulfilling in many ways. I think that the human being has, I've always felt that an individual has a kind of capacity for happiness. If his aspirations are fairly well met he's a happy person. And of course people in those days did not aspire to an awful lot they didn't have any more than they do today. People always want something they don't have. They're probably the relatively quiet life that people enjoyed in life.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: We were talking about the kind of life that people enjoyed in those days. I was mentioning the fact that life was built around home life. You had the conveniences that you were accustomed to. You didn't always have the same that you're aspired to. I think the same thing is true today. You may be less frustrated because you were less aware of better things elsewhere. You didn't get around to see what other people had so much. You lived at home. Now in a town like Bovill there wasn't really that wide a difference between the way one family lived and another.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: But people, as I say, people have a capacity to be happy and they were happy under it. I can't today think that I enjoy life at all more because I have...

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Let's say a better... better conveniences around me and that sort of thing than I did in those days with the conveniences I had then which was all I knew about and was used to having. There was an enchantment about the times. It's part of this old sentiment that you have for the past.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: It's kind of hard to describe but I guess that's at the bottom of it all. People worked awfully hard. I do know that, for example, my mother's life, her work day would absolutely amaze women today. I just can't imagine a woman today working the way she worked. I know she got very tired of the long past but she never complained about it and she was happy. When she got old she was very thankful that she didn't have to work as hard as she used to. I think if she had been young again she would have gone right back into the old schedule and would have been just as happy as ever. Perhaps in many ways she might have been happier with the old life than the new. I think most people might. Because they have a... it interests them and it's a thing that they kind of feel that it's right.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: There are many things they would not like to go back to. But they would probably face it and do just like the people then did then. They would struggle to make things better.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: For example, when I was young, I'll just give you an example of what kind of a day my mother might have. She was probably up at no later than five in the morning building a fire. First thing, she and my dad would take the milk pails out to the barn to milk the cows and she would milk her by probably six cows before breakfast. Come in and make breakfast. Like as not the two girls might wash the dishes at that stage. But her and my dad's job then was delivery of milk through town. Not only milk but maybe garden vegetables and meats and things like that as well. So that would take perhaps the next two hours in the morning. Go biking and go around house to house and make the deliveries. And then she had the laundry to do, the cooking to do. In canning season she probably put up hundreds of jars of fruits and vegetables. She used to can meats in the same way. Every time we were butchered she'd maybe can a couple hundred jars of beef.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: We carried our water from probably 300 feet from the house. She had probably 200 milk bottles a day to wash from the milk deliveries. Had to carry all the water to do it to the house. There was no water piping. In the earlier days there was no water piping.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I don't know how she did it but she found time to help put up hay, cut wood, clear land out there with my dad in the field grubbing out stumps and trying to make hay fields.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Whenever she wasn't busy in the house she was doing that.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: We never had enough clothing to buy new clothing when they were worn. It was patched, the socks were darned and things like that. She'd sit there by a kerosene lamp light, darning socks or patching clothes until 10 o 'clock at night. From 4 in the morning until 10 o 'clock at night I don't think she ever rested. She was one of the fastest people I ever saw to get work done. Work just fell behind at an amazing rate. She was quick, very rapid.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Women today can't do it, won't do it or can't do it but I don't think many of them know how.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I don't think many men today can keep up with the schedule that a farmer uses.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Physically they probably couldn't. Take a lumberjack out on a crosscut saw.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Did you ever swing a crosscut saw?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Your shoulder would get tired. The lumberjacks did it all day long, 12 hours a day. We talked about this guy Pete Olson. I remember the year that he and I cut hay out. It was the year after the forest fire out on Little Meadow there. My dad brought Timothy in among the ashes there. We had Timothy, I guess it would have reached up to that wind chime there which is standing about 5 feet from the ground. It was very heavy but it was all in among the stumps and the litter and the logs. We needed the hay and Pete Olson came out with his scythe and I got my scythe and we were in there for 2 or 3 weeks with the scythe just going around cutting that stuff. It was at least 3 weeks and we had to carry it out. There was so much litter on the ground that we would get the pitchfork and get a fork full and we might have to carry it 100 yards before we could get it to where we could load it on a slip or a wagon to haul it to the barn. We put up about 8 or 10 tons all hand cut and all carried out by hand among the stumps. You finish a summer that way you're in pretty good physical shape.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Men today could do it but they have to get in physical shape for it. They're not used to it but the majority of them aren't used to doing that. Still there is a lot of physical work in the woods.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Tractor logging for example. You cut a log up a steep hillside like that and these men that grab one of these cables and hooks and run up that hillside and they'll often go up at a run dragging that cable behind them to hook onto a log and drag it down and put you in shape.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: These people like that life except for the fact that they wouldn't have television and washing machines and things like that at home. It wouldn't be too alien to them. But I suspect among farm women you still have some people that work very hard. But generally speaking city women have forgotten what work is relatively.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: The turning point as I see it in the town was the Depression years which more or less marked the time of the exhaustion of the large stands of prime timber near the town. After that they had to step way back into the headwaters, lots of times over the divide into the clear water drainage and that sort of thing for the extensive amounts of timber. There were little patches of big timber left here and there but nothing very substantial.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: The Depression put a stop on the logging that had existed and it more or less coincided with the time of the exhaustion of that kind of timber supplies. It went straight on in through from there into the war when timber was needed but there was a limited opportunity to log in a big way because of the war situation. They were coming out of it during the war. The logging did start to expand again but it expanded in a new pattern. They started a railroad out of town and the grade runs up south of town there and it was to go on out through the east fork and across into the clear water. During the early war years they started that thing and somehow or other they realized that logging was going to turn around and rails were not going to be any longer the way of moving logs. So they backed up and built a crushed rock road. You see it led bridges from the end of the lush logging of the 1920s into the Depression and then into a new type of logging that began about during the war and stepped back much farther for the primary supplies of timber. Another thing that happened, I think this was about just after the war that was a rather crucial thing for the town, was the moving the high school out. I can't date this in my own mind. I was in South America at the time and I don't have precise dates in time but I know that her older daughter went to high school in Deary already. They were riding buses to Deary at that time. The merging of the school districts, I have a feeling that it was very bad for the Bovill community. For example being put in with Troy, this end of the county has always been outvoted by the farmer down at Troy. The Bovill interests have kind of lagged through the political set up that arises from that kind of a merger. But particularly Deary getting the high school instead of Bovill has hurt the town of Bovill quite seriously.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Deary as a pivotal middle is really more like Troy than it is like Bovill isn't it?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Well, it's kind of in between in a way. It's less dependent on farming perhaps than Troy but yes, I would say the Deary outlook is probably more like a Troy outlook than it is like a Bovill outlook. It is based more on an agricultural type rather than a town dwellers. Most of the Bovill people are town dwellers. They're dependent on wages for their living. You have gone from the time of continuous logging year round logging operations to a time when the logging is very seasonal. Then another thing of course with the exhaustion of the tremendous supplies of cedar, there's still cedar around but not in the supplies that once had. The demand for poles probably slacked off partly from the availability of poles and partly from the availability of new metal type poles for suspending wires. Of course, a tremendous trend toward going underground with wiring or using no wiring at all. I just use electronic systems for communications.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Transmitted waves. There is not the demand nor the supply of poles. You have to realize that Bovill in the past at least through the 1920s this pole industry here was tremendous.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Now this cedar yard here where it used to be is dead completely. What is left of it is up above the Y up north of town. The only thing they use here is a treating plant anymore. I believe it's still in use. It was last year. There's new ownership this year I understand but I don't quite know how they're operating.

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the composition of the population in Bovill has changed very much?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Yes, it's changed quite a lot.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I feel that there probably... I have a feeling that all of the public throughout the land there has been a degeneration in a way of moral fiber. Seems to me there has and I'm fully aware of the fact that historians have always claimed this is so. There certainly are ups and downs through time but I think we've gone through one of the downs at least in recent years. You can see it in Bovill as you can in other places.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: What does it mean in actual...

speaker: Rob Moore or Sam Schrager
words: to the town?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I think there is a much more lax view towards say things like marriage and that sort of thing. But as I say this has gone throughout the whole land and it strikes Bovill like it does every place else. You do see I think a larger percentage of people around the town today that seem to have very little interest in keeping up their places and maintaining a nice and neat looking home than there used to be. Yet some of the homes are as nice as ever were. But it just seems to me that there is a larger number of people that have become shoddy in the way of doing things. This is part of what I call moral fiber of the community.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Or of the people in general. I wonder if it doesn't reflect the difference in attitudes towards work.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: We've been talking about the tremendous amount of work that people used to do and expect to do. That's right. The existence of social programs, the existence of government support of the needy and that sort of thing is very real. The need for such a thing is very real but there is no doubt that it is abused and the existence of such a thing seems to demoralize certain types of people. Make them unwilling to do for themselves what they think the government will do for them. This is one of the bad factors and one of the bad features of having programs of that kind. I don't deny the need for such programs but people become less progressive when they feel that things might be furnished anyway.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: You have to look at it as good I think anytime when you provide ways of properly taking care of your old people, that part, you certainly have to. I don't think anybody can help but underwrite that part of the program.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: But where do you go from there?

speaker: John B. Miller
words: I don't quite know.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Certainly there are some people that really are needy and there are other people who manage to become needy if it's possible.

speaker: John B. Miller
words: Reviewing that I would say that the beginning of the Depression and the exhaustion of the large stands of timber coming, coinciding more or less, that's probably the most critical point of all in the bubble history. And since the building of the town itself.
